steps.title: Factor the quadratic
steps.content: To factor $3x^2 + 20x - 63$, look for two binomials $(3x + a)(x + b)$ where $ab = -63$ and $3b + a = 20$. Testing $a = -7, b = 9$: $(3x - 7)(x + 9) = 3x^2 + 27x - 7x - 63 = 3x^2 + 20x - 63$. ✓

steps.title: Test expression I using the Factor Theorem
steps.content:
By the Factor Theorem, $(x - 9)$ is a factor if and only if $f(9) = 0$.
$$f(9) = 3(9)^2 + 20(9) - 63 = 243 + 180 - 63 = 360$$
Since $f(9) = 360 \neq 0$, expression I is NOT a factor.

steps.title: Test expression II using the Factor Theorem
steps.content:
By the Factor Theorem, $(3x - 7)$ is a factor if and only if $f(7/3) = 0$ (setting $3x - 7 = 0$ gives $x = 7/3$).
$$f(7/3) = 3\left(\frac{7}{3}\right)^2 + 20\left(\frac{7}{3}\right) - 63 = \frac{49}{3} + \frac{140}{3} - 63 = \frac{189}{3} - 63 = 0$$
Since $f(7/3) = 0$, expression II IS a factor. Only statement II is a factor. That matches choice B — B is correct.

choiceAnalysis:
• Choice A — 'I only': Expression I is $(x - 9)$. Testing $f(9) = 3(81) + 180 - 63 = 360 \neq 0$, so $x - 9$ is not a factor. ✗
  • Choice B — 'II only': Expression II is $(3x - 7)$. Testing $f(7/3) = 49/3 + 140/3 - 63 = 0$ ✓. Expression I fails as shown. Only II is a factor. ✓
  • Choice C — 'I and II': Expression I is not a factor (f(9) = 360), so both cannot be factors. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Neither I nor II': Expression II is confirmed a factor via the Factor Theorem. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
